meta.title: GCP-PDE-2: Architecture, BigQuery and Dataflow Operations

domains.name: Preparing and Using Data for Analysis

questions.stem: Your company processes IoT sensor data arriving at 50,000 events per second. The data must be processed in real-time for anomaly detection and also stored for historical analysis. Which Google Cloud architecture best satisfies both requirements?

options.text: Publish events to Pub/Sub, use Dataflow for streaming processing and anomaly detection, write results to BigQuery for historical analysis
Stream events directly into BigQuery using the streaming insert API, then query with scheduled jobs for anomaly detection
Ingest events into Cloud Storage, use Cloud Composer to trigger Dataproc batch jobs for anomaly detection
Use Cloud Spanner as the primary ingestion layer to handle the write throughput, then export to BigQuery nightly

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct. Pub/Sub decouples ingestion from processing and handles bursty high-throughput workloads at 50,000 events/second. Dataflow (Apache Beam) provides a unified streaming model for real-time anomaly detection with exactly-once semantics. BigQuery accepts Dataflow output for durable, low-cost historical analysis. This is the canonical GCP pattern for dual real-time and historical requirements.

**B** is incorrect. Streaming inserts into BigQuery bypass a real-time processing layer entirely. Scheduled queries for anomaly detection introduce significant latency and cannot satisfy real-time requirements.

**C** is incorrect. Staging events in Cloud Storage then running Dataproc batch jobs introduces batch latency. There is no real-time anomaly detection capability in this architecture.

**D** is incorrect. Cloud Spanner is a transactional relational database, not designed for high-throughput IoT event ingestion. Nightly exports to BigQuery do not satisfy the real-time processing requirement.

[Pub/Sub overview](https://cloud.google.com/pubsub/docs/overview)

questions.stem: A financial services company needs a database solution that supports multi-region strong consistency, ACID transactions across rows and tables, and SQL querying. Which Google Cloud product should be used?

options.text: Cloud Bigtable
Cloud Spanner
Firestore in Datastore mode
Cloud SQL with read replicas

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct. Cloud Spanner is the only Google Cloud database that provides globally distributed, horizontally scalable, strongly consistent, ACID-transactional relational storage with standard SQL support. Its TrueTime API enables external consistency across regions, satisfying all three requirements.

**A** is incorrect. Cloud Bigtable is a NoSQL wide-column store optimized for high-throughput reads and writes. It does not support ACID transactions across rows or SQL querying.

**C** is incorrect. Firestore is a NoSQL document database. While it supports atomic operations within a single document or limited transaction scope, it does not provide multi-region strong consistency at the relational level or full SQL support.

**D** is incorrect. Cloud SQL is a managed relational database but it is single-region primary by design. Read replicas provide horizontal read scaling but do not offer multi-region strong consistency; failover replicas have replication lag.

[Cloud Spanner overview](https://cloud.google.com/spanner/docs/overview)

questions.stem: You are designing a data lake on Google Cloud. Raw files of varying formats (JSON, CSV, Avro, Parquet) arrive daily. You need cost-effective storage with the ability to run ad-hoc queries without loading data first. Which approach is best?

options.text: Store files in Cloud Storage and use BigQuery external tables to query them in place
Load all files into Cloud Bigtable and query using HBase API
Convert all files to CSV and load into Cloud SQL
Use Dataflow to pre-process files and load into Firestore before querying

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct. BigQuery external tables allow querying data directly in Cloud Storage across CSV, JSON, Avro, Parquet, and ORC formats without ingestion or ETL. Cloud Storage provides durable, low-cost object storage ideal for a data lake landing zone. This eliminates loading costs for exploratory analysis.

**B** is incorrect. Cloud Bigtable does not accept arbitrary file formats and requires data to be written via its proprietary HBase-compatible API. It is optimized for time-series and wide-column access patterns, not ad-hoc SQL analytics.

**C** is incorrect. Converting all formats to CSV and loading into Cloud SQL requires an ETL step before any query, violating the requirement. Cloud SQL is also limited in scalability for large data lake volumes.

**D** is incorrect. Firestore is a document database designed for transactional application data, not analytical querying. Pre-processing via Dataflow before every query adds pipeline complexity and latency.

[BigQuery external tables](https://cloud.google.com/bigquery/docs/external-tables)

questions.stem: A Dataflow streaming pipeline is experiencing high watermark lag. The pipeline reads from Pub/Sub, performs a windowed aggregation with a 5-minute fixed window, and writes to BigQuery. What is the most likely cause and remediation?

options.text: The BigQuery write sink is the bottleneck; switch from streaming inserts to Storage Write API batch mode
The Pub/Sub subscription has too many undelivered messages; increase Dataflow worker count and set maxNumWorkers
Late-arriving data is holding the watermark back; set an allowed lateness and trigger early firings with accumulatingFiredPanes mode
Windowed aggregation is not supported in streaming mode; switch to Dataproc Spark Structured Streaming

questions.explanation:
**C** is correct. Watermark lag in windowed Dataflow pipelines is typically caused by late-arriving data. The pipeline holds the watermark open waiting for out-of-order events before emitting the window result. Setting `allowedLateness` with `ACCUMULATING_FIRED_PANES` allows the pipeline to emit early results and update on late arrivals, keeping the watermark advancing and reducing lag.

**A** is incorrect. BigQuery write sink throughput affects output latency but does not cause watermark lag. Watermark lag is a property of the input data ordering, not the output sink speed.

**B** is incorrect. Increasing worker count addresses throughput (reducing backlog of unprocessed messages) but does not advance the watermark. The watermark is determined by the timestamps of events, not the number of workers.

**D** is incorrect. Windowed aggregations are fully supported in Dataflow streaming mode. This is a core Apache Beam feature and one of the primary use cases for Dataflow.

[Dataflow watermarks and late data](https://cloud.google.com/dataflow/docs/concepts/streaming-pipelines#watermarks)

questions.stem: You need to migrate 200 TB of on-premises relational data to BigQuery with minimal downtime. The data resides in PostgreSQL. Which Google Cloud service provides continuous replication (CDC) to keep the target synchronized during the migration cutover window?

options.text: Datastream for BigQuery
Transfer Appliance for physical shipment followed by bulk load
BigQuery Data Transfer Service with Cloud SQL connector
Cloud Composer DAG using PostgreSQL operator to export and load daily

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct. Datastream is Google Cloud's fully managed CDC and replication service. It reads the PostgreSQL WAL (write-ahead log) and replicates changes (INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE) directly into BigQuery in near-real-time. It handles initial backfill and continuous replication simultaneously, enabling a low-downtime cutover for migrations targeting BigQuery.

**B** is incorrect. Transfer Appliance is for offline physical data transfer when network bandwidth is insufficient for initial load, but it provides no ongoing CDC capability and does not target BigQuery directly.

**C** is incorrect. BigQuery Data Transfer Service handles SaaS data sources (Google Ads, YouTube, etc.) and scheduled BigQuery-to-BigQuery copies. It does not support CDC from Cloud SQL or on-premises PostgreSQL.

**D** is incorrect. A Cloud Composer DAG polling PostgreSQL daily is a batch approach that introduces up to 24 hours of data lag. It does not minimize downtime during cutover and is operationally fragile for 200 TB migrations.

[Datastream for BigQuery](https://cloud.google.com/datastream/docs/bigquery-destination)

questions.stem: A Dataflow batch pipeline processes files from Cloud Storage and writes results to BigQuery. Jobs are failing intermittently with OutOfMemoryError on workers. The pipeline performs a large CoGroupByKey operation. What should you do?

options.text: Increase the number of workers using --numWorkers flag
Enable Dataflow Shuffle service to offload shuffle data off worker memory
Rewrite the pipeline to use Cloud Dataproc instead
Increase worker disk size using --diskSizeGb flag

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct. Dataflow Shuffle service (`--experiments=shuffle_mode=service`) offloads the shuffle phase to a managed backend, removing intermediate shuffle data from worker JVM heap memory. This is the recommended solution for OOM errors caused by large `GroupByKey` or `CoGroupByKey` operations, as it eliminates memory pressure from accumulating shuffle state.

**A** is incorrect. Increasing worker count distributes the overall data volume but does not reduce per-key memory requirements when keys are skewed or when a single key's grouped value set exceeds one worker's heap. OOM errors on individual workers persist.

**C** is incorrect. Rewriting to Dataproc Spark introduces significant rework and is not warranted before exhausting Dataflow optimization options. Dataflow Shuffle service directly addresses the OOM issue.

**D** is incorrect. Increasing disk size expands storage for spill files but does not help JVM heap OOM errors. Shuffle data is held in memory before spilling, and OOM occurs before disk is utilized.

[Dataflow Shuffle service](https://cloud.google.com/dataflow/docs/guides/deploying-a-pipeline#cloud-dataflow-shuffle)

questions.stem: You need to run a recurring ETL pipeline that extracts data from Cloud Storage, transforms it using PySpark, and loads it into BigQuery. The job runs once daily and must minimize cost. Which service should you use?

options.text: Dataproc on a persistent cluster with scheduled jobs
Dataproc with ephemeral cluster creation per job run, using preemptible VMs
Dataflow with a batch pipeline triggered by Cloud Scheduler
Cloud Run jobs executing a PySpark container

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct. Dataproc with ephemeral clusters (create at job start, delete at completion) charges only for active job runtime. Using preemptible (Spot) VMs for worker nodes further reduces cost by up to 80%. Since the job runs once daily, there is no benefit to a persistent cluster sitting idle 23+ hours per day.

**A** is incorrect. A persistent Dataproc cluster incurs continuous VM costs even when no jobs are running. For a once-daily job this wastes significant budget on idle time.

**C** is incorrect. Dataflow is also a valid choice for scheduled batch jobs, but it requires rewriting PySpark code to Apache Beam. The question specifies existing PySpark code, so Dataproc avoids a costly rewrite.

**D** is incorrect. Cloud Run is a container execution platform optimized for stateless web services and tasks. PySpark requires a JVM-based Spark cluster with distributed workers, which Cloud Run does not provide natively.

[Dataproc ephemeral clusters](https://cloud.google.com/dataproc/docs/concepts/jobs/overview)

questions.stem: Your team uses Apache Kafka on-premises for event streaming. You are migrating to Google Cloud. Which two Google Cloud services can act as a managed replacement for Kafka topics with minimal application code changes? (Select TWO)

options.text: Cloud Pub/Sub
Cloud Pub/Sub Lite
Cloud Bigtable
Managed Apache Kafka on Google Cloud

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct. Cloud Pub/Sub is GCP's fully managed message broker with pub/sub semantics (at-least-once delivery, fan-out to multiple subscriptions). The Pub/Sub Kafka connector allows existing Kafka producers and consumers to publish and subscribe with minimal code changes.

**D** is correct. Managed Service for Apache Kafka on Google Cloud provides native Kafka API compatibility, allowing existing Kafka producers and consumers to connect without any code changes. It supports Kafka protocols, consumer group offsets, and topic semantics natively.

**B** is incorrect. Pub/Sub Lite is a lower-cost, reduced-feature variant of Pub/Sub with zonal availability and manual partition management. While it can replace Kafka, it offers less automatic scaling than standard Pub/Sub and has more operational overhead. It is not the preferred option over standard Pub/Sub for a minimal-change migration.

**C** is incorrect. Cloud Bigtable is a NoSQL wide-column database optimized for time-series and analytics workloads. It does not function as a message queue or event streaming platform and cannot replace Kafka topics.

[Managed Apache Kafka on Google Cloud](https://cloud.google.com/managed-kafka/docs/overview)

questions.stem: A Dataflow streaming pipeline uses session windows to group user events. Sessions close after 30 minutes of user inactivity. You notice some sessions never close and the pipeline accumulates unbounded state. What is the root cause?

options.text: Session windows are not supported in streaming mode; use sliding windows instead
The Pub/Sub subscription is not acknowledging messages, causing re-delivery that resets window timers
Missing allowedLateness configuration means Beam never garbage-collects closed window state
A small subset of users generates a continuous stream of events with no gaps, preventing their session window from ever closing

questions.explanation:
**D** is correct. Session windows close only when no events arrive within the gap duration (30 minutes). If certain users generate continuous high-frequency events without a 30-minute gap, their session window extends indefinitely, accumulating state. This is a fundamental property of session windowing. Solutions include capping session duration with a maximum session length or using fixed windows for high-frequency users.

**A** is incorrect. Session windows are fully supported in Dataflow streaming mode and are one of the primary use cases for Apache Beam's windowing model.

**B** is incorrect. Pub/Sub re-delivery causes duplicate messages but does not reset window timers. Window timers in Beam are driven by event timestamps and the watermark, not by Pub/Sub delivery mechanics.

**C** is incorrect. `allowedLateness` controls how long Beam retains state for late data after a window closes. It would affect garbage collection of closed windows, but the described problem is windows that never close in the first place, not cleanup of already-closed windows.

[Session windows in Apache Beam](https://beam.apache.org/documentation/programming-guide/#session-windows)

questions.stem: You want to schedule a Cloud Composer (Apache Airflow) DAG to run every day at 6 AM UTC and send an alert email if any task fails. Which Airflow feature handles the failure notification?

options.text: SLA miss callbacks at the DAG level
The on_failure_callback parameter on tasks or email_on_failure=True with email configuration
Cloud Monitoring alerting policies triggered by Airflow log entries
Pub/Sub topic subscription bound to the Airflow task_failed event

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct. Airflow's `on_failure_callback` or `email_on_failure=True` with SMTP configuration is the built-in mechanism for task failure notifications. Setting `email_on_failure=True` and providing email addresses in `default_args` causes Airflow to send an alert automatically when any task in the DAG fails. This is the idiomatic Airflow approach requiring no external services.

**A** is incorrect. SLA miss callbacks trigger when a task or DAG misses its expected completion time (SLA), not specifically on task failure. They serve a different monitoring purpose.

**C** is incorrect. Cloud Monitoring can alert on Airflow logs but requires additional configuration to parse log entries for failure patterns. It is not the idiomatic Airflow failure notification mechanism and adds operational complexity.

**D** is incorrect. Airflow does not publish task events to Pub/Sub topics by default. Building such an integration would require custom code and is far more complex than the built-in `email_on_failure` parameter.

[Cloud Composer email alerts](https://cloud.google.com/composer/docs/how-to/managing/alerting)

questions.stem: You need to store time-series metrics from 10,000 IoT devices, with each device writing one record per second. Queries access data for a single device over a time range. Which storage system is optimal?

options.text: Cloud Spanner
Cloud Firestore
Cloud SQL
Cloud Bigtable

questions.explanation:
**D** is correct. Cloud Bigtable is purpose-built for time-series workloads. It handles millions of reads/writes per second, stores data in lexicographically sorted rows, and supports range scans over row keys. Designing the row key as `deviceId#timestamp` provides efficient time-range scans for a single device. At 10,000 devices × 1 write/second = 10,000 writes/second, Bigtable handles this with ease.

**A** is incorrect. Cloud Spanner supports fewer writes per second relative to Bigtable at this scale and is more expensive. It is designed for globally consistent transactional workloads, not high-throughput time-series ingestion.

**B** is incorrect. Cloud Firestore is a document database designed for user-facing application data with moderate throughput. It is not optimized for high-write time-series data or efficient time-range scans.

**C** is incorrect. Cloud SQL is a managed relational database with limited write throughput compared to Bigtable. At 10,000 writes per second it would require significant sharding and would not be cost-effective for time-series storage.

[Cloud Bigtable for time series](https://cloud.google.com/bigtable/docs/schema-design-time-series)

questions.stem: A BigQuery table is partitioned by ingestion date. Queries that filter on a specific date column (event_date) are not pruning partitions and scan the full table. What is the most likely cause?

options.text: BigQuery only prunes partitions when the filter uses _PARTITIONDATE or _PARTITIONTIME pseudo-columns
The table uses ingestion-time partitioning but the query filters on event_date, a different column; partitions are pruned only on the partition column
The partition expiration is set too low, causing BigQuery to bypass pruning
Queries must use TABLESAMPLE to enable partition pruning

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct. Ingestion-time partitioned tables use `_PARTITIONTIME` (the load timestamp) as the partition key, not any data column. Filtering on `event_date` (a column in the table's schema) does not match the partition key, so BigQuery cannot prune partitions and scans all data. To prune on `event_date`, convert the table to column-based partitioning on that column.

**A** is incorrect. While `_PARTITIONDATE` and `_PARTITIONTIME` are the correct pseudo-columns for ingestion-time partitioning, the question identifies a different root cause: the filter is on a data column (`event_date`), not a partition pseudo-column at all. Option A describes a correct but incomplete picture.

**C** is incorrect. Partition expiration controls when old partitions are deleted, not whether partition pruning is applied during query execution. Expiration settings do not affect pruning logic.

**D** is incorrect. `TABLESAMPLE` is used to sample a fraction of table rows for statistical exploration. It is unrelated to partition pruning and does not enable or disable it.

[BigQuery partitioned tables](https://cloud.google.com/bigquery/docs/partitioned-tables)

questions.stem:
You need to choose between Cloud Storage storage classes for the following data:

- ML training datasets accessed approximately once per month
- Compliance archives accessed once per year

Which storage classes minimize cost for each respectively?

options.text: Standard for training datasets; Archive for compliance archives
Nearline for training datasets; Archive for compliance archives
Standard for both
Nearline for training datasets; Coldline for compliance archives

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct. Nearline storage is optimized for data accessed less than once per month with a 30-day minimum storage duration and a per-access retrieval fee. Data accessed approximately once per month sits at the Nearline break-even point and is typically cheaper than Standard. Archive class has the lowest storage cost (roughly 8x cheaper than Standard) and is designed for data accessed less than once per year, making it ideal for compliance archives with annual access.

**A** is incorrect. Standard storage for monthly-access training data is more expensive in storage costs than Nearline when access frequency is once per month or less. Archive is correctly chosen for compliance archives.

**C** is incorrect. Standard is appropriate only for frequently accessed data (daily or more). Using Standard for annual-access compliance archives wastes budget on unnecessarily high storage costs.

**D** is incorrect. Coldline is designed for data accessed less than once per quarter with a 90-day minimum storage duration. For data accessed only once per year, Archive provides lower storage cost with a 365-day minimum duration that matches the access pattern.

[Cloud Storage storage classes](https://cloud.google.com/storage/docs/storage-classes)

questions.stem: A BigQuery dataset contains a table with 500 billion rows growing by 50 million rows per day. Query performance on recent data has degraded. The table is clustered on user_id and partitioned by date. Analysts primarily query the last 30 days, filtered by user_id. What should you do first?

options.text: Add a second clustering column for event_type to narrow scans further
Enable BigQuery BI Engine to cache frequently accessed partitions
Check clustering effectiveness with INFORMATION_SCHEMA.TABLE_STORAGE; if blocks are fragmented, run a re-clustering job
Migrate the table to Cloud Bigtable for better range scan performance

questions.explanation:
**C** is correct. BigQuery automatically re-clusters tables on a best-effort basis, but high write rates on very large tables can cause clustering fragmentation over time. Checking `INFORMATION_SCHEMA.TABLE_STORAGE` reveals block metrics and clustering health. For heavily fragmented tables, a `CREATE OR REPLACE TABLE ... AS SELECT ... CLUSTER BY user_id` operation re-clusters the data. This diagnostic-first approach addresses the root cause before adding system complexity.

**A** is incorrect. Adding a second clustering column could help but only if queries also filter on `event_type`. Adding complexity without diagnosing the actual performance degradation is premature optimization.

**B** is incorrect. BI Engine caches hot data for sub-second dashboard queries, not for general query performance. It does not address clustering fragmentation or scan efficiency for arbitrary analytical queries.

**D** is incorrect. Cloud Bigtable is optimized for key-value and time-series workloads, not the complex analytical SQL queries analysts run on BigQuery. Migrating a 500-billion-row analytical table to Bigtable is unnecessary and counterproductive.

[BigQuery clustering](https://cloud.google.com/bigquery/docs/clustered-tables)

questions.stem: You need to store semi-structured JSON documents for a user profile service. Profiles are queried by user_id and sub-fields are accessed in application code. The workload is 90% reads. Which Google Cloud database is most suitable?

options.text: Cloud Bigtable
Cloud Firestore (Native mode)
Cloud Spanner with JSON column type
BigQuery with JSON column type

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct. Firestore in Native mode is a fully managed NoSQL document database designed for semi-structured document storage. It provides single-document strong consistency, real-time listeners, sub-collections, and scales to millions of concurrent reads. It is purpose-built for application-facing workloads like user profiles with high read ratios.

**A** is incorrect. Cloud Bigtable stores data as sparse rows with column families, not as native JSON documents. While Bigtable can store JSON as bytes in a cell, it lacks native document indexing, sub-field access patterns, and the application-friendly client libraries that Firestore provides.

**C** is incorrect. Cloud Spanner is a globally distributed relational database designed for transactional consistency. While it supports JSON columns, it is significantly more expensive than Firestore for application-facing profile lookups and adds unnecessary complexity for a document-centric use case.

**D** is incorrect. BigQuery is an OLAP data warehouse optimized for large-scale analytical queries, not for low-latency point lookups by user_id. Query latency is typically seconds, making it unsuitable for user-facing profile service reads.

[Cloud Firestore overview](https://cloud.google.com/firestore/docs/overview)

questions.stem: You need to build an interactive dashboard with sub-second query response times on a BigQuery table with 10 billion rows. Users filter by region and date range. Which feature should you enable?

options.text: BigQuery Reservations (slots)
BigQuery BI Engine
Materialized views with auto-refresh
Connected Sheets

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct. BigQuery BI Engine is an in-memory analysis service that caches hot data and query results for sub-second query response times. It is designed specifically for dashboard workloads and integrates transparently with Looker Studio, Looker, and direct BigQuery queries. It accelerates queries with common filter patterns like region and date range.

**A** is incorrect. BigQuery Reservations (slot commitments) guarantee dedicated compute capacity, improving throughput and reducing query queue time. However, they do not reduce individual query execution time to sub-second for 10-billion-row scans.

**C** is incorrect. Materialized views pre-compute and cache query results for specific query patterns. They help when queries match the materialized view's definition exactly, but they do not provide sub-second latency for arbitrary filter combinations on a 10-billion-row table.

**D** is incorrect. Connected Sheets connects Google Sheets to BigQuery for spreadsheet-based analysis. It executes queries each time and is not designed for sub-second interactive dashboard performance.

[BigQuery BI Engine](https://cloud.google.com/bigquery/docs/bi-engine-intro)

questions.stem: A data analyst wants to run SQL queries on a BigQuery table but should not be able to see the credit_card_number or ssn columns. What is the recommended approach using native BigQuery features?

options.text: Create an authorized view that excludes sensitive columns and grant the analyst access to the view, not the base table
Use column-level security with policy tags and assign the analyst a role without the associated data policy binding
Encrypt the sensitive columns with Cloud KMS and do not provide the analyst with decryption keys
Delete the sensitive columns and recreate them in a separate restricted table

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct. BigQuery column-level security uses policy tags defined in Data Catalog applied to sensitive columns. Access to tagged columns is controlled by IAM bindings on the policy tag taxonomy. Users without the `Fine-Grained Reader` role on the policy tag receive an access denied error when querying those columns. This is the native, scalable approach that enforces security at the data layer regardless of which tool or view is used to access the table.

**A** is incorrect. Authorized views work and are a valid option, but they require maintenance as the schema evolves (adding columns, renaming, etc.) and rely on correct view definition. Column-level security with policy tags is enforced at the table level and is more robust and scalable across multiple consumers.

**C** is incorrect. Cloud KMS encryption secures data at rest at the storage level. BigQuery decrypts data automatically for authorized users during query execution, so withholding KMS keys from analysts does not prevent them from reading decrypted values via SQL.

**D** is incorrect. Deleting production columns to create a restricted table is destructive, requires schema duplication, and creates data consistency risks. It is not a scalable or maintainable security approach.

[BigQuery column-level security](https://cloud.google.com/bigquery/docs/column-level-security)

questions.stem: You are building a feature engineering pipeline for a machine learning model. Raw features include numeric values, categorical strings, and timestamp fields. Which Google Cloud service provides managed feature transformation and storage for training and serving consistency?

options.text: Vertex AI Feature Store
Cloud Dataprep
Dataflow with TensorFlow Transform
BigQuery ML feature preprocessing

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct. Vertex AI Feature Store provides a centralized repository for storing, sharing, and serving ML features. It eliminates training-serving skew by using the same feature values at training time (offline serving via BigQuery export) and inference time (online serving via low-latency API). It handles feature versioning, monitoring, and reuse across models.

**B** is incorrect. Cloud Dataprep is a visual data preparation tool for cleaning and transforming tabular data before analysis. It is not designed for ML feature serving or for eliminating training-serving skew between batch training and real-time inference.

**C** is incorrect. Dataflow with TensorFlow Transform is a valid approach for computing feature transformations at scale and can export preprocessing functions for serving consistency. However, it does not provide a managed feature serving layer or feature store capabilities for online inference.

**D** is incorrect. BigQuery ML supports in-database ML training with feature transformations using SQL `TRANSFORM` clauses. However, it does not provide a feature serving API for low-latency online inference or a centralized feature store for cross-model feature sharing.

[Vertex AI Feature Store](https://cloud.google.com/vertex-ai/docs/featurestore/overview)

questions.stem: A BigQuery query joins two large tables (10 billion rows and 500 million rows) and takes 45 minutes. The EXPLAIN plan shows a shuffle join. The smaller table is 8 GB. What should you do to improve performance?

options.text: Partition both tables on the join key column to enable partition pruning during the join
Force a broadcast join by restructuring the query so the smaller table appears first in the FROM clause
Pre-aggregate the 500 million row table into a materialized view and join against the view
Enable BigQuery BI Engine to cache the join result for sub-second repeated queries

questions.explanation:
**C** is correct. Pre-aggregating the 500 million row table in a materialized view reduces its cardinality significantly before the join. Joining against a much smaller aggregated result set reduces the shuffle volume and join cost. This is the most effective optimization when the join itself is the bottleneck and the smaller side cannot be broadcast.

**A** is incorrect. Partitioning helps with row pruning when filtering on partition columns, but it does not reduce the cost of joining two already-large tables if the join is not filtered by partition key. The join itself would still scan all relevant partitions.

**B** is incorrect. BigQuery's query optimizer determines join strategy based on table statistics, not FROM clause order. At 8 GB, the smaller table exceeds the typical broadcast join threshold (~1 GB), so BigQuery correctly uses a shuffle join. Reordering the FROM clause does not force a broadcast join.

**D** is incorrect. BI Engine accelerates repeated queries against cached data for dashboards. It does not reduce the execution time of a single expensive analytical join, and a 45-minute query likely exceeds BI Engine's scope.

[BigQuery query optimization](https://cloud.google.com/bigquery/docs/best-practices-performance-compute)

questions.stem: You want to use Vertex AI AutoML to build a tabular classification model. Your dataset has 100,000 rows and 50 features. Three features contain 40% null values. What should you do before training?

options.text: Remove the three features with missing values; AutoML cannot handle nulls
Impute missing values with the column mean before uploading the dataset
AutoML Tables handles missing values automatically; review feature importance after training to decide whether to impute or drop
Increase the dataset to at least 1 million rows before training; 100K is too small for AutoML

questions.explanation:
**C** is correct. Vertex AI AutoML for tabular data handles missing values natively as part of its automated feature engineering. It can learn from the absence pattern itself (missingness as a signal). The best practice is to train first, then review the feature importance report. If features with 40% nullity have low importance, they can be dropped in a subsequent training run.

**A** is incorrect. AutoML does not require removal of features with null values. Dropping features before training discards potentially useful signal and should only be done based on feature importance results.

**B** is incorrect. Mean imputation before uploading is valid but unnecessary for AutoML, which handles imputation automatically. Manual imputation can also introduce bias if not done carefully.

**D** is incorrect. Vertex AI AutoML for tabular data requires a minimum of 1,000 rows for training. 100,000 rows is well within acceptable limits and sufficient for many classification tasks.

[Vertex AI AutoML tabular data](https://cloud.google.com/vertex-ai/docs/tabular-data/classification-regression/overview)

questions.stem: A Dataflow streaming job must be updated to add new processing logic without losing any in-flight Pub/Sub messages or state. What is the correct procedure?

options.text: Cancel the job, update the pipeline code, and start a new job from the latest Pub/Sub snapshot
Use Dataflow job update (--update flag) with a compatible pipeline graph to replace the job in-place
Deploy the new code as a separate job, drain the old job, then redirect Pub/Sub traffic to the new subscription
Pause the job with the drain command, update the template, and resume

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct. Dataflow supports in-place streaming job updates via the `--update` flag, provided the new pipeline graph is compatible with the running graph (same transform names and step types). The update preserves in-flight state and workers, transitions seamlessly, and does not require a Pub/Sub snapshot or new subscription creation. This is the recommended zero-downtime method.

**A** is incorrect. Cancelling a streaming job drops all in-flight messages that have been read from Pub/Sub but not yet processed. Starting from a Pub/Sub snapshot can help recover acknowledged messages but is complex and risks data loss for messages in-flight at cancel time.

**C** is incorrect. Deploying a parallel job and draining the old one works but requires creating a new Pub/Sub subscription, duplicating infrastructure, and managing the traffic redirect. It is more complex and error-prone than using `--update`.

**D** is incorrect. The Dataflow drain command is used to gracefully stop a job (processing all buffered data and closing windows), not to pause it. Once drained, the job terminates and cannot be resumed with a template update.

[Updating Dataflow pipelines](https://cloud.google.com/dataflow/docs/guides/updating-a-pipeline)

questions.stem: You need to monitor BigQuery slot utilization across projects in your organization and alert when utilization exceeds 80% for more than 5 minutes. Which approach should you use?

options.text: Query INFORMATION_SCHEMA.JOBS_BY_ORGANIZATION every minute from a Cloud Scheduler + Cloud Functions job
Use Cloud Monitoring with the bigquery.googleapis.com/storage/table_count metric and set a threshold alert
Enable BigQuery Admin resource charts, export metrics to Cloud Monitoring, and create an alerting policy on the slot utilization metric
Use Looker Studio to build a dashboard showing slot utilization with a 5-minute refresh

questions.explanation:
**C** is correct. BigQuery exports slot utilization metrics (`bigquery.googleapis.com/reservation/slot_utilization`) to Cloud Monitoring automatically. Creating an alerting policy with a 5-minute alignment period and an 80% threshold condition sends notifications via configured channels (email, PagerDuty, Slack, etc.). This is the native, low-latency, alerting-capable approach at organization scale.

**A** is incorrect. Polling `INFORMATION_SCHEMA.JOBS_BY_ORGANIZATION` every minute via Cloud Functions adds unnecessary operational complexity, query costs, and cannot detect sustained utilization over a configurable time window as precisely as Cloud Monitoring alerting policies.

**B** is incorrect. `bigquery.googleapis.com/storage/table_count` is a storage metric tracking number of tables, not slot utilization. This metric is unrelated to compute resource monitoring.

**D** is incorrect. Looker Studio dashboards are for visualization, not automated alerting. A 5-minute refresh dashboard requires a human to observe it and cannot trigger automated notifications when thresholds are breached.

[BigQuery monitoring with Cloud Monitoring](https://cloud.google.com/bigquery/docs/monitoring)

questions.stem: A daily Dataproc job fails sporadically with 'YARN container killed due to exceeded memory' errors on worker nodes. The job runs identical code each day. What is the most likely cause?

options.text: Dataproc automatically scales down workers during the job, reducing available memory
The YARN container memory limit is too low; increase yarn.nodemanager.resource.memory-mb in cluster configuration
Dataproc does not support YARN resource management; switch to Kubernetes-based execution
Input data volume varies daily, causing memory pressure on days with larger files

questions.explanation:
**D** is correct. Sporadic failures with identical code strongly indicate data volume variation as the root cause. If input files are larger on some days (e.g., higher transaction volume, backlog ingestion), per-task memory requirements exceed the YARN container limit. The fix is to enable Dataproc autoscaling to add workers under memory pressure, increase the worker machine type, or investigate and cap partition sizes upstream.

**A** is incorrect. Dataproc autoscaling (if enabled) can scale down workers based on YARN resource metrics, but it scales down only when resources are underutilized. If a job is failing with OOM errors, autoscaling would not trigger a scale-down during active job execution.

**B** is incorrect. If the YARN memory limit were consistently too low, the job would fail every day. Sporadic failures rule out a static configuration issue as the primary cause.

**C** is incorrect. Dataproc fully supports YARN as its resource manager for Hadoop and Spark workloads. Switching to Kubernetes (Dataproc on GKE) does not resolve memory issues caused by variable data volumes.

[Dataproc autoscaling](https://cloud.google.com/dataproc/docs/concepts/configuring-clusters/autoscaling)

questions.stem: Your organization requires that all BigQuery query results be retained for 90 days for audit purposes. Queries run across multiple projects. What is the most scalable implementation?

options.text: Enable BigQuery audit logs to Cloud Logging and create a log sink to a Cloud Storage bucket with 90-day retention
Use BigQuery query history in the BigQuery UI, which retains 180 days of history automatically
Write a Cloud Functions trigger on every BigQuery job completion that copies results to a separate audit table
Enable INFORMATION_SCHEMA.JOBS_BY_ORGANIZATION export to a BigQuery audit dataset with a 90-day table expiration

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct. Enabling Data Access audit logs (`DATA_READ` and `DATA_WRITE` log types for BigQuery) captures all queries and results organization-wide. An organization-level log sink to Cloud Storage with an object lifecycle rule for 90-day retention provides durable, scalable, low-cost audit storage. This single sink captures logs across all projects in the organization without per-project configuration.

**B** is incorrect. Query history in the BigQuery UI is for interactive user exploration, not a compliance-grade audit mechanism. It is not enforceable, not centralized across all projects, and the retention period cannot be configured to exactly 90 days.

**C** is incorrect. A Cloud Functions trigger per BigQuery job completion is complex, operationally fragile, and does not scale well to organization-wide query volumes. It also cannot capture the query result content directly since results are returned to the caller, not a function trigger.

**D** is incorrect. `INFORMATION_SCHEMA.JOBS_BY_ORGANIZATION` is a read-only view of job metadata, not a data export destination. There is no built-in mechanism to automatically export its contents to a dataset with configurable retention.

[BigQuery audit logs](https://cloud.google.com/bigquery/docs/reference/auditlogs)

questions.stem: You are selecting a messaging service for a pipeline where exactly-once delivery semantics are critical and consumers need to replay messages from any offset. Which service best fits these requirements?

options.text: Cloud Pub/Sub standard topics
Cloud Pub/Sub with exactly-once delivery enabled
Cloud Pub/Sub with message ordering and a committed offset store
Managed Apache Kafka on Google Cloud

questions.explanation:
**D** is correct. Apache Kafka provides native consumer group offsets stored in the broker, allowing consumers to seek to any offset and replay messages from any point in the retained log. Kafka's exactly-once semantics (idempotent producers and transactional APIs) satisfies both requirements natively. Managed Apache Kafka on Google Cloud provides these capabilities without operational overhead.

**A** is incorrect. Cloud Pub/Sub standard topics provide at-least-once delivery with no offset replay capability. Subscriptions only deliver messages from when they were created or from a snapshot; arbitrary offset seeking is not supported.

**B** is incorrect. Cloud Pub/Sub exactly-once delivery (available since 2022) prevents duplicate delivery within a subscription but does not support arbitrary offset replay. Once a message is acknowledged, it cannot be replayed from a specific offset.

**C** is incorrect. Pub/Sub with message ordering guarantees ordered delivery per ordering key but still does not provide seekable offsets. A custom committed offset store adds complexity without providing native replay capabilities comparable to Kafka.

[Managed Apache Kafka on Google Cloud](https://cloud.google.com/managed-kafka/docs/overview)

questions.stem: A Dataflow pipeline reading from Cloud Storage is processing files much slower than expected. The input is 50,000 small files (each ~10 KB). What is the primary performance issue?

options.text: Dataflow cannot process more than 10,000 files per job
The pipeline lacks sufficient workers; set --numWorkers=100
Small file overhead: each file open/close incurs Cloud Storage API calls, creating excessive overhead per record
Cloud Storage does not support parallel reads from many small files

questions.explanation:
**C** is correct. Processing many small files is the classic 'small files problem'. Each file requires at least one Cloud Storage API call for metadata and open operations. With 50,000 files of ~10 KB each (500 MB total data), API overhead vastly outweighs actual data processing. The fix is to compact small files into larger files (100–500 MB each) upstream, or use `gsutil compose` / Storage Compose to merge them before Dataflow processing.

**A** is incorrect. Dataflow has no hard limit of 10,000 files per job. It can process millions of input files, though performance degrades as described due to API overhead.

**B** is incorrect. Adding workers increases parallelism but does not reduce the per-file API overhead. Each worker still incurs an API call per file it processes, so the overhead scales with worker count.

**D** is incorrect. Cloud Storage fully supports parallel reads from many files simultaneously. The issue is not a Cloud Storage capability limitation but the overhead ratio of API calls to data volume.

[Dataflow performance best practices](https://cloud.google.com/dataflow/docs/guides/deploying-a-pipeline#best-practices)

questions.stem: You need to design a Cloud Bigtable row key for a table storing user click events. The access pattern is: retrieve all events for a specific user in a time range. Which row key design is best?

options.text: timestamp#user_id
user_id#timestamp
A UUID (random) as the row key
event_type#user_id#timestamp

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct. Bigtable stores rows in lexicographic order. Using `user_id#timestamp` places all events for a single user in contiguous rows. A range scan from `user_id#start_time` to `user_id#end_time` retrieves exactly the desired data efficiently. This design collocates a user's events on the same tablet, minimizing remote tablet reads.

**A** is incorrect. `timestamp#user_id` groups all events at a given timestamp together across all users. This creates write hotspots on the latest timestamp prefix and does not support efficient per-user time-range scans without scanning all users' data.

**C** is incorrect. Random UUIDs distribute writes evenly across tablets (avoiding hotspots) but make it impossible to perform range scans for a specific user's events. Every query would require a full table scan.

**D** is incorrect. `event_type#user_id#timestamp` groups events by type first. While this supports queries like 'all click events for user X', the access pattern specifies retrieving all event types for a user in a time range, making `event_type` as the leading key counterproductive.

[Cloud Bigtable schema design](https://cloud.google.com/bigtable/docs/schema-design)

questions.stem: A data scientist needs to explore and clean a 50 GB CSV dataset before loading it into BigQuery. They prefer a visual, no-code interface. Which Google Cloud service is most appropriate?

options.text: Cloud Dataprep by Trifacta
Vertex AI Workbench (Jupyter notebooks)
BigQuery Data Transfer Service
Cloud Dataflow

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct. Cloud Dataprep (powered by Alteryx/Trifacta) is Google Cloud's intelligent visual data preparation service. It provides a no-code UI for exploring, cleaning, and transforming data. It automatically detects schemas, profiles data quality, suggests transformations based on patterns, and can output cleaned data directly to BigQuery.

**B** is incorrect. Vertex AI Workbench provides Jupyter notebooks for code-based data science work. While powerful, it requires Python or R coding skills and is not a no-code visual interface for data preparation.

**C** is incorrect. BigQuery Data Transfer Service automates data movement from SaaS applications and other Google services into BigQuery. It is not a data exploration or cleaning tool.

**D** is incorrect. Cloud Dataflow requires writing Apache Beam pipeline code (Python or Java) to transform data. It is designed for large-scale production ETL pipelines, not interactive visual data exploration.

[Cloud Dataprep overview](https://cloud.google.com/dataprep/docs/html/Dataprep-Overview_57375369)

questions.stem: You need to implement CDC (Change Data Capture) from a Cloud SQL for PostgreSQL database into BigQuery with sub-minute latency. Which service provides this natively?

options.text: BigQuery Data Transfer Service with Cloud SQL connector
Datastream for BigQuery
Cloud Composer DAG polling Cloud SQL every minute
Database Migration Service with continuous replication

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct. Datastream is Google Cloud's fully managed CDC and replication service. Datastream for BigQuery reads the PostgreSQL WAL (write-ahead log) and replicates INSERT, UPDATE, and DELETE operations directly into BigQuery in near-real-time (typically sub-minute latency). It handles schema evolution and initial backfill automatically, making it the native solution for this use case.

**A** is incorrect. BigQuery Data Transfer Service handles SaaS data sources (Google Ads, YouTube Analytics, etc.) and does not support CDC from Cloud SQL or PostgreSQL. There is no Cloud SQL connector for continuous replication.

**C** is incorrect. A Cloud Composer DAG polling Cloud SQL every minute is a batch approach, not CDC. It would require tracking changed rows via a timestamp column, miss deletes, and introduce up to one minute of latency per cycle—not sub-minute for all changes.

**D** is incorrect. Database Migration Service (DMS) is designed for migrating databases to Cloud SQL, AlloyDB, or other managed targets. It replicates to database destinations, not directly to BigQuery, and is not the appropriate tool for ongoing operational CDC to an analytics store.

[Datastream for BigQuery](https://cloud.google.com/datastream/docs/bigquery-destination)

questions.stem: A BigQuery table stores orders. Analysts frequently query the last 90 days and filter by customer_region. The table has 5 years of data. Which combination of table features minimizes query cost?

options.text: Partition by order_date, cluster by customer_region
Cluster by order_date, partition by customer_region
Partition by customer_region, cluster by order_date
No partitioning; use materialized views aggregated by region

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct. Partitioning by `order_date` allows BigQuery to skip all partitions outside the 90-day window, dramatically reducing bytes scanned from 5 years of data to 90 days. Clustering by `customer_region` then sorts data within each partition, further reducing bytes scanned for region filters. This is the canonical pattern: partition on the time dimension for range queries, cluster on the most-filtered categorical column.

**B** is incorrect. BigQuery does not support clustering on a date column as the primary key without partitioning. Partitioning by `customer_region` (a low-cardinality string) would create ineffective partitions since there are few distinct regions and each partition would still span all 5 years of data.

**C** is incorrect. Partitioning by `customer_region` does not help with the dominant filter (last 90 days). Querying the last 90 days would still scan all years of data within each region partition, defeating the primary cost optimization goal.

**D** is incorrect. Materialized views with region aggregations pre-compute summaries but do not reduce scan costs for arbitrary queries that need row-level data. They also add storage cost and require maintenance.

[BigQuery partitioning and clustering](https://cloud.google.com/bigquery/docs/partitioned-tables)

questions.stem: You need to share a BigQuery dataset with an external partner company. They should be able to query the data but cannot copy it to their own project. Which approach enforces this?

options.text: Grant the partner bigquery.dataViewer on the dataset and require their service account to sign a DUA
Use Analytics Hub to publish the dataset as a listing; the partner subscribes and gets a linked dataset they can query but not export
Create a Cloud Storage export nightly and share the bucket with the partner's project
Use VPC Service Controls to prevent the partner from copying data

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct. Analytics Hub allows data publishers to share BigQuery datasets as listings. Subscribers get a linked dataset in their own project — they can query the data but cannot export tables to Cloud Storage, copy tables to their project, or run EXPORT DATA statements. This is the purpose-built GCP solution for governed, read-only cross-organization data sharing.

**A** is incorrect. Granting `bigquery.dataViewer` on the dataset allows the partner to query AND export data (via `EXPORT DATA` statements) to their own Cloud Storage bucket. Standard IAM roles do not prevent data exfiltration. A DUA is a legal agreement, not a technical control.

**C** is incorrect. Exporting to Cloud Storage and sharing the bucket gives the partner a full copy of the data, completely violating the requirement to prevent data copying.

**D** is incorrect. VPC Service Controls can restrict which networks and identities access BigQuery resources within a perimeter. However, configuring a service perimeter around the partner's access to prevent data export without breaking legitimate query access is complex and not the purpose-built solution for cross-organization data sharing.

[Analytics Hub overview](https://cloud.google.com/bigquery/docs/analytics-hub-introduction)

questions.stem: Your team wants to implement data quality checks on BigQuery tables after each daily ETL pipeline run. Which Google Cloud service provides managed, declarative data quality rules for BigQuery without writing custom code?

options.text: Dataplex Auto Data Quality
Cloud Monitoring custom metrics
BigQuery scheduled queries with assertion checks
Cloud Data Catalog tag templates

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct. Dataplex Auto Data Quality allows you to define declarative data quality rules (completeness, uniqueness, validity, referential integrity, custom SQL) on BigQuery tables and schedule them to run automatically. Results are stored in BigQuery and viewable in the Dataplex UI with no custom code required. It integrates with Cloud Composer for pipeline orchestration.

**B** is incorrect. Cloud Monitoring custom metrics track infrastructure and application performance metrics. They do not inspect data content within BigQuery tables to evaluate completeness, uniqueness, or validity rules.

**C** is incorrect. BigQuery scheduled queries can run assertion checks via SQL but require writing and maintaining custom SQL assertion logic, managing result tables, and building alerting separately. This is not a managed, declarative solution.

**D** is incorrect. Cloud Data Catalog tag templates attach metadata (business descriptions, ownership, classifications) to data assets. They are for data discovery and governance metadata, not for executing data quality validation rules against table content.

[Dataplex Auto Data Quality](https://cloud.google.com/dataplex/docs/data-quality-overview)

questions.stem: You are building a real-time fraud detection system. Events must be scored within 200ms. The model is trained in Vertex AI. Which serving approach meets the latency requirement?

options.text: Batch prediction jobs run every hour, results stored in BigQuery for lookup
Vertex AI online prediction endpoint with the model deployed to an endpoint
Dataflow pipeline calling a model deployed on Vertex AI every 5 minutes
Export the model to Cloud Storage and load it in a Cloud Run service for inference

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct. Vertex AI online prediction endpoints provide synchronous, low-latency (typically 10–100ms) model serving via REST or gRPC API. For real-time fraud scoring requiring sub-200ms response times, this is the standard GCP pattern. Endpoints support autoscaling, A/B testing, and monitoring out of the box.

**A** is incorrect. Batch prediction jobs run asynchronously and process large datasets offline. Results are available only after job completion (minutes to hours), making them entirely unsuitable for real-time per-transaction fraud scoring.

**C** is incorrect. A Dataflow pipeline with a 5-minute micro-batch interval has up to 5 minutes of latency per event. This fails the 200ms requirement by orders of magnitude.

**D** is incorrect. Exporting a model to Cloud Run is a valid custom serving approach and can meet sub-200ms latency, but it requires manual management of the serving container, autoscaling configuration, and monitoring. Vertex AI endpoints provide these capabilities as managed services with less operational overhead.

[Vertex AI online prediction](https://cloud.google.com/vertex-ai/docs/predictions/online-predictions-custom-models)

questions.stem: A Cloud Composer DAG orchestrates a multi-step pipeline. One task downloads data from an external API that is rate-limited to 10 requests per minute. The task exceeds the limit and fails. What is the best way to handle this in Airflow?

options.text: Set pool_slots=1 and create a pool with 10 slots for API tasks to limit concurrency
Use retries=3 with retry_delay=timedelta(minutes=1) to space out retries
Add a BashOperator sleep(60) task before the API task
Set max_active_runs=1 to prevent multiple DAG runs from executing simultaneously

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct. Airflow Pools limit concurrent task execution across DAG runs. Creating a pool (e.g., `external_api_pool`) with 10 slots and assigning all API tasks to that pool ensures no more than 10 concurrent API requests are made at any time, respecting the rate limit. This control is enforced across multiple DAG runs and task instances simultaneously.

**B** is incorrect. Retries with a delay handle failures after rate limiting occurs, but they do not prevent the rate limit from being exceeded in the first place. If multiple parallel task instances are running, they will all hit the rate limit and all retry, continuing to exceed it.

**C** is incorrect. A sleep before the API task delays execution but does not control concurrent access. Multiple parallel task instances would still sleep and then all hit the API simultaneously.

**D** is incorrect. `max_active_runs=1` limits concurrent DAG runs but does not limit concurrent tasks within a run. If the API task is called multiple times within a single DAG run, all instances would still execute concurrently.

[Airflow Pools](https://airflow.apache.org/docs/apache-airflow/stable/administration-and-deployment/pools.html)

questions.stem: You need to detect PII (personally identifiable information) in unstructured text documents stored in Cloud Storage and redact it before loading into BigQuery. Which Google Cloud service automates this?

options.text: Cloud Data Loss Prevention (DLP) API with deidentification
Vertex AI Natural Language API entity extraction
BigQuery Data Masking with column-level security
Cloud KMS with envelope encryption of Cloud Storage objects

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct. Cloud DLP (now Sensitive Data Protection) provides automated PII detection with 150+ built-in infoTypes (SSN, credit cards, email addresses, phone numbers, passport numbers, etc.) and deidentification transformations (redaction, masking, tokenization, format-preserving encryption). It can inspect Cloud Storage objects directly and write deidentified output to a destination bucket before BigQuery ingestion.

**B** is incorrect. Vertex AI Natural Language API identifies named entities (persons, organizations, locations) but does not specifically detect PII infoTypes and does not provide deidentification or redaction transformations. It returns entity labels, not redacted text.

**C** is incorrect. BigQuery Data Masking (column-level security with dynamic data masking) applies masking rules at query time for data already in BigQuery. It does not redact data before it enters BigQuery and does not process unstructured text in Cloud Storage.

**D** is incorrect. Cloud KMS envelope encryption protects data at rest by encrypting Cloud Storage objects. It does not detect or redact PII content within documents; authorized users with decryption keys can still read the raw PII.

[Sensitive Data Protection (Cloud DLP)](https://cloud.google.com/sensitive-data-protection/docs/deidentify-sensitive-data)

questions.stem: You need to enforce that BigQuery table data in the eu-west1 region never leaves the EU boundary. Which Google Cloud feature enforces this at the infrastructure level?

options.text: IAM conditions restricting access to EU IP ranges
VPC Service Controls perimeter around the BigQuery project alone
BigQuery dataset location set to EU combined with a VPC Service Controls service perimeter
Cloud Armor security policy blocking non-EU traffic

questions.explanation:
**C** is correct. Setting the BigQuery dataset location to EU (or a specific EU region like `europe-west1`) ensures data storage does not leave EU infrastructure. VPC Service Controls adds a security perimeter that restricts which identities and networks can access the dataset via API calls, preventing exfiltration to outside the perimeter. Both controls together provide comprehensive data residency and access boundary enforcement.

**A** is incorrect. IAM conditions can restrict access based on request attributes (like resource tags or time), but IP range conditions in IAM do not prevent users with valid credentials from accessing data via the API from outside the EU. IAM conditions alone are not a data residency control.

**B** is incorrect. A VPC Service Controls perimeter alone controls API access boundaries but does not control where BigQuery physically stores the data. Without setting the dataset location to EU, data could be stored in any region.

**D** is incorrect. Cloud Armor is a DDoS protection and web application firewall service for HTTP(S) load balancers. BigQuery is accessed via its own API endpoint, not through a load balancer that Cloud Armor protects. Cloud Armor does not apply to BigQuery data access.

[BigQuery data residency](https://cloud.google.com/bigquery/docs/locations)

questions.stem: A Dataflow job processes Pub/Sub messages but occasionally processes the same message twice, causing duplicate records in BigQuery. What should you implement to fix this?

options.text: Enable Dataflow exactly-once mode (--experiments=enable_exactly_once_delivery) and use BigQuery Storage Write API in committed mode
Increase the Pub/Sub subscription ack deadline to prevent re-delivery
Add a deduplication step using a GroupByKey on message_id before writing to BigQuery
Switch to Pub/Sub Lite which guarantees exactly-once delivery

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct. Dataflow streaming exactly-once mode (available on Dataflow Runner V2) combined with BigQuery Storage Write API in COMMITTED mode provides end-to-end exactly-once processing. Dataflow guarantees exactly-once semantics within the pipeline graph; the Storage Write API provides idempotent commits via stream offsets, preventing duplicate writes to BigQuery even during retries.

**B** is incorrect. Increasing the Pub/Sub ack deadline reduces re-delivery due to slow processing but does not eliminate duplicates caused by Dataflow worker failures, checkpointing, or exactly-once violations. Duplicates can still occur when workers restart.

**C** is incorrect. A `GroupByKey` on `message_id` for deduplication adds significant state overhead, increases memory pressure, and introduces latency. It also does not provide true exactly-once guarantees since the deduplication window is finite.

**D** is incorrect. Pub/Sub Lite provides at-least-once delivery, not exactly-once. It is a lower-cost, reduced-feature variant of Pub/Sub and does not offer stronger delivery guarantees than standard Pub/Sub.

[Dataflow exactly-once processing](https://cloud.google.com/dataflow/docs/concepts/exactly-once)

questions.stem: Your organization wants to implement a governed data mesh architecture on Google Cloud. Each domain team should own and publish their data products independently. Which Google Cloud service combination best supports this architecture?

options.text: BigQuery authorized views shared via individual dataset IAM bindings
Dataplex for data governance and Analytics Hub for data product publishing
Cloud Data Catalog for metadata management across all domain datasets
A central Cloud Composer DAG orchestrating all domain data pipelines

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct. A data mesh requires domain ownership, data product publishing, and federated governance. Dataplex provides the governance layer: each domain team manages their data lake zone in Dataplex with data quality rules, lineage tracking, and metadata management. Analytics Hub provides the data product marketplace layer: domain teams publish listings that consumers discover and subscribe to. Together they implement the GCP-native data mesh platform.

**A** is incorrect. Authorized views with IAM bindings provide basic access control but do not support the data product discovery, subscription model, or federated governance required for a data mesh. Managing individual IAM bindings at scale across many domain teams is operationally complex.

**C** is incorrect. Cloud Data Catalog provides metadata management, lineage, and data discovery across domains. However, it is a governance and discovery tool, not a data product publishing platform. It does not support the self-service subscription model required for a data mesh marketplace.

**D** is incorrect. A central Cloud Composer DAG directly contradicts the data mesh principle of domain ownership and decentralized data pipelines. Centralizing orchestration creates a bottleneck and makes domain teams dependent on a shared platform team for pipeline changes.

[Dataplex overview](https://cloud.google.com/dataplex/docs/introduction)

questions.stem: Which Apache Beam primitive is responsible for applying a user-defined function to each element in a PCollection?

options.text: GroupByKey
Flatten
ParDo
CoGroupByKey

questions.explanation:
**C** is correct. `ParDo` (Parallel Do) is the fundamental element-wise transform in Apache Beam. It applies a `DoFn` (user-defined function) to each element of a PCollection, optionally emitting zero or more output elements per input element. It is the primary mechanism for data transformation, filtering, and enrichment in Beam pipelines.

**A** is incorrect. `GroupByKey` is a Beam transform that groups all elements with the same key together into a single key-value pair where the value is an iterable of all matching elements. It is used for aggregation, not element-wise transformation.

**B** is incorrect. `Flatten` merges multiple PCollections of the same type into a single PCollection. It is used to union parallel branches of a pipeline, not to apply user-defined functions to individual elements.

**D** is incorrect. `CoGroupByKey` performs a relational join-like operation, grouping elements from multiple keyed PCollections by their common keys. It is used for joining data streams, not for applying element-wise user-defined functions.

[Apache Beam ParDo transform](https://beam.apache.org/documentation/programming-guide/#pardo)

questions.stem: You need to implement automated rollback for a Dataflow streaming pipeline if error rate exceeds 5% within a 10-minute window after deployment. What is the recommended approach?

options.text: Use Cloud Monitoring alerting with a notification channel that triggers a Cloud Function to run the Dataflow job update API with the previous template version
Maintain a manual runbook and rely on on-call engineers to monitor and roll back if needed
Use Cloud Deploy with Dataflow Flex Templates and configure automated rollback targets
Set Dataflow's --maxNumWorkers=0 to pause processing when errors are detected

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct. Cloud Monitoring can track Dataflow pipeline error rate metrics with a 10-minute alignment window. An alerting policy with a 5% error rate threshold triggers a Cloud Function via a Pub/Sub notification channel. The Cloud Function calls the Dataflow `jobs.update` API with the previous Flex Template version to perform an automated rollback. This is the recommended GCP pattern for automated pipeline rollback.

**B** is incorrect. Manual runbooks rely on human response time, which cannot meet the 10-minute detection window consistently. Human monitoring introduces variability and is unsuitable for automated rollback requirements.

**C** is incorrect. Cloud Deploy supports deployment of Dataflow Flex Templates and provides rollback targets for pipeline versions. However, automated rollback based on runtime error rate metrics requires integration with Cloud Monitoring alerting, not a built-in feature of Cloud Deploy at this time.

**D** is incorrect. Setting `--maxNumWorkers=0` is not a valid Dataflow operation for pausing a streaming pipeline. Dataflow streaming pipelines require at least one worker to maintain state and process messages. The minimum is 1 worker.

[Cloud Monitoring alerting policies](https://cloud.google.com/monitoring/alerts/using-alerting-policies)
